item.title: Sadtu slams as ‘cancerous’ the selling of EC teaching posts
item.dc:creator: Abongile Solundwana
item.description:
The Eastern Cape chapter of the SA Democratic Teachers Union (Sadtu) has condemned the practice of selling posts in schools in the province as a “cancer” that is undermining the region’s education system. At the Themba Kojana induction of regional leaders and shop stewards on Tuesday, SADTU provincial chair Mazotsho Dukwe said the union was […]

The post Sadtu slams as ‘cancerous’ the selling of EC teaching posts appeared first on The Rep.
item.content:encoded:
The Eastern Cape chapter of the SA Democratic Teachers Union (Sadtu) has condemned the practice of selling posts in schools in the province as a “cancer” that is undermining the region’s education system.

At the Themba Kojana induction of regional leaders and shop stewards on Tuesday, SADTU provincial chair Mazotsho Dukwe said the union was aware of cases where people were being charged exorbitant amounts of money for teaching and management positions in schools.

“People are being charged R25,000 for a level one post, R50,000 for an HOD post, and the amount ranges from R70,000 for a deputy post to R100,000 for a principal post.

This is corruption at its worst, and it’s going to hold us back,” Dukwe said.

The chair added that the selling of posts was not only limited to educators but involved the school governing body (SGB) parents who were making money out of it.

“SGB members are sitting on panels to appoint principals, deputies and educators, and they’re getting corrupted. We want to do away with this.”

Dukwe warned that the practice was having a negative impact on the province’s education system as those who bought posts often underperformed.

“If someone buys a post they’re not supposed to have, it means someone else, who is qualified, might have lost out on the position.

“It means deserving candidates will never get posts in the Eastern Cape if they don’t have money or aren’t willing to pay for them.”

The Sadtu leader called on society to condemn the practice, saying that it is undermining the province’s efforts to improve education.

He also said the union had succeeded in the pushing for the appointment of 870 Grade R educators, and that they were close to filling the positions, although there was still a slight shortage as the number needed was 950.

“Previously Grade R teachers were treated as casual workers.

There is going to be a change in their conditions of work. They will now qualify for medical aid, housing subsidy and a 13th cheque.” Dukwe said.

Despite these challenges, Dukwe praised the province’s educators for their sterling work in improving matric results.

“We are applauding our members for the sterling work done with matric results to ensure that our province is now at a safer level compared to 10 and 15 years ago. We were somewhere at 59% or so, and today we have achieved 84.17%.”

The chair highlighted the province’s achievements, including the government intervention in the aviation school in Port Alfred that now offers pupils the opportunity to train as pilots.

“When we visited the school before there was no South African in the school, nor any learner from the Eastern Cape,” he said.

“Those who were at the school came from Sweden, Russia, Egypt and Arab Emirates.

“We’re appreciating the role played by Premier Oscar Mabuyane by affording a scholarship to a cohort of 25 learners last year.

“We’re proud to know that we have potential pilots in our province. ”

ABONGILE SOLUNDWANA

The post Sadtu slams as ‘cancerous’ the selling of EC teaching posts appeared first on The Rep.

item.title: Local playwright ‘becoming’ in the theatre circles debut
item.dc:creator: Abongile Solundwana
item.description:
Hlalanathi Panya (stage name “HP”), a 20-year-old playwright from Komani, is making his debut at the Baxter Theatre with “Becoming”, premiering from March 14 to 19. Panya, who’s pursuing a diploma in performing arts at Northlink College, draws inspiration from personal experience. The play’s idea emerged when a friend (Rue Jansen) encouraged him to write […]

The post Local playwright ‘becoming’ in the theatre circles debut appeared first on The Rep.
item.content:encoded:
Hlalanathi Panya (stage name “HP”), a 20-year-old playwright from Komani, is making his debut at the Baxter Theatre with “Becoming”, premiering from March 14 to 19.

Panya, who’s pursuing a diploma in performing arts at Northlink College, draws inspiration from personal experience.

The play’s idea emerged when a friend (Rue Jansen) encouraged him to write something authentic — advice that sparked his creative journey.

Theatre wasn’t Panya’s firstlove —film was. “I chose film because I was surrounded by scenes every day but I do enjoy theatre because it’s way more difficult to create,” he laughs.

He used to make short clips in high school at Queen’s College, but struggled with finding people and getting a quiet day.

He admires the power of storytelling in its rawest form.

“When it comes to theatre, you work with nothing and make it something,” he says.

“Becoming” weaves between two timelines —the 1940s- 1950s and the behind-the scenes reality of the 2020s in South Africa.

“I hope the audience gains insight into the challenges faced by actors,” Panya says.

Maintaining consistency was the most challenging part of bringing the play to life.

“Films are one-take things, but stage is a never-ending process,” he says.

The playwright credits his team for helping him navigate these challenges.

“The stage is like a universal language. You might not understand the language, but the actions and intention are clear and emotionally resonant.

“It brings you closer to home, while developing crucial skills needed for theatre.

“The industry, though, has the potential to make you lose yourself. Mental health is crucial when you’re a creative.”

To get “Becoming” staged, Panya reveals the hard part was securing the full production.

“People love an idea, but execution is a different story. That was my biggest challenge,” he says.

Recognition is great, but Panya’s focus is on making an impact. “I hope it connects with
people and sparks change. If not, I’ll take it as a lesson learned. It’s all about the audience in this industry.”

ABONGILE SOLUNDWANA

The post Local playwright ‘becoming’ in the theatre circles debut appeared first on The Rep.

item.title: Hughes inspires young Karatekas after earning his Junior 1st Degree Black Belt
item.dc:creator: sphokuhle nongauza
item.description:
Tom ’s Dojo recently celebrated a proud milestone as long-serving karateka Nathan Hughes successfully graded to his Junior 1st Degree Black Belt following a demanding and highly physical assessment in Komani. The black belt grading, hosted by Tom’s Dojo, tested Hughes across 10 different disciplines in what is regarded as one of the most challenging […]

The post Hughes inspires young Karatekas after earning his Junior 1st Degree Black Belt appeared first on The Rep.
item.content:encoded:
Tom ’s Dojo recently celebrated a proud milestone as long-serving karateka Nathan Hughes successfully graded to his Junior 1st Degree Black Belt following a demanding and highly physical assessment in Komani.

The black belt grading, hosted by Tom’s Dojo, tested Hughes across 10 different disciplines in what is regarded as one of the most challenging milestones in martial arts progression.

Unlike regular gradings, a black belt assessment is a full-contact fighting examination that pushes candidates physically and mentally.

Kyoshi Tom Hayes described the achievement as well deserved, highlighting Hughes’ years of dedication to the sport.

“Nathan has been training karate for many years. He has been very loyal and committed to his club and style. He has grown from strength to strength in his techniques, strength and fitness,” said Hayes.

Hughes, who serves as a Sempai at the dojo —a senior-ranking student who assists in teaching others — demonstrated exceptional skill and discipline throughout the grading process.

“He has shown exceptional skills which he has both mastered and tested. The black belt grading is very strenuous. It is very physical and also a great test of one’s mental strength,” Hayes explained.

Sensei Shahil Harry, who was also part of the grading panel, joined Hayes in confirming that Hughes had passed with flying colours.

“We are exceptionally proud of Nathan. He is a great ambassador of Tom’s Dojo,” Hayes added.

The achievement marks another step forward in Hughes’ martial arts journey, reinforcing his role as both a dedicated student and a mentor within the dojo.

Tom ’s Dojo continues to develop disciplined and skilled martial artists, with Hughes’ accomplishment standing as an inspiration to younger students aspiring to reach black belt level.

SPHOKUHLE NONGAUZA

The post Hughes inspires young Karatekas after earning his Junior 1st Degree Black Belt appeared first on The Rep.

item.title: 51st Surfers brings wet weather
item.dc:creator: Rep Reporter
item.description:
BOB NORRIS There have been few wet weather Surfers challenge races down the years and the only thing that is certain for the 51st running of the Discovery Surfers Challenge from Kwelera Mouth to Nahoon Beach on Saturday is that there will be weather. At the time of writing, the forecast suggests good rain on […]

The post 51st Surfers brings wet weather appeared first on The Rep.
item.content:encoded:
BOB NORRIS

There have been few wet weather Surfers challenge races down the years and the only thing that is certain for the 51st running of the Discovery Surfers Challenge from Kwelera Mouth to Nahoon Beach on Saturday is that there will be weather.

At the time of writing, the forecast suggests good rain on Saturday morning with a continuation of rain throughout the day and temperatures in the region of 23°C.

Rain and running work well together, though paddling out on the ocean for the men and women of the sea could be more affected by visibility.

That said, there are rocks to contend with on the 17.5km run from the traditional start close to the Yellow Sands Resort and through Rainbow Valley, Sunrise-on-Sea, crossing the Gonubie River onto Bonza Bay and other easier, though softer surfaces to negotiate.

February is recognised as the hottest month though strong winds have also been experienced as was the case earlier this week.

Surfers’ contestants are generally a hardy bunch and will take whatever is dealt them.

One of the features of running or paddling Surfers is the warm welcome received by many hundreds of spectators at the Nahoon Beach finish for both, while the runners receive great enthusiasm all the way from the lively start, through the various landmarks and particularly through the beautiful suburb of Gonubie which endures for roughly 6km, from the time runners emerge from the river until they disappear down a tricky bush path back onto the beach at Eastward Ho.

The people are fantastic and the biggest roar comes at the Gonubie Hotel corner where many congregate and cheer all on with a great deal of ferocity.

It is also the start of the shorter event, the contestants of which will have somewhat churned up the sand though to Bonza Bay and Nahoon when the Kwelera brigade arrive.

There have been many outstanding athletes participate, inclusive of at least one Olym-pic runner who heard of the quaint race, which started as a challenge between genuine surfers and old runners, many of them known in such circles as purely “tar treaders”.

Comrades runners the likes of Gordon Shaw, Alan Robb, Bruce Fordyce, Danny Biggs and Shaun Meiklejohn have created interest down the years.

Shaw and Biggs were the only two to win the race, while Meiklejohn joined in the latter years and challenged well.

The original Surfers was run over 16.5km and only Mzwandile Shube and Michael Scout, both of whom have passed on, broke the 60 min barrier with times of 55:40 and 59:19 respectively.

The distance was upped to 17.5km in 2010 and since then Zingisile Marikeni has been the fastest in 66:30.

The fastest women have been the outstanding Hanlie Botha and Myrette Brink, who hails from Pretoria, while in the early days Colleen de Reuck was the most impressive.

Conditions of weather and the terrain and the flow of the Gonubie and Nahoon Rivers have determined the times and on some days they have delivered a hard day at the office.

Final entry lists are still being compiled, but with no road races impacting the calender at the weekend one or two fast running stars could pop up and challenge the records.

The post 51st Surfers brings wet weather appeared first on The Rep.

item.title: Suspect arrrested for cable theft in Queendustria
item.dc:creator: Abongile Solundwana
item.description:
A suspect is in police custody after being caught stealing municipal electricity cable in Queendustria, while two others fled the scene around 12pm today. A local business owner witnessed three men digging up the road, exposing the power cable, leading to the arrest. This follows a similar incident in October last year where four […]

The post Suspect arrrested for cable theft in Queendustria appeared first on The Rep.
item.content:encoded:
A suspect is in police custody after being caught stealing municipal electricity cable in Queendustria, while two others fled the scene around 12pm today. A local business owner witnessed three men digging up the road, exposing the power cable, leading to the arrest. This follows a similar incident in October last year where four suspects were arrested, and the case is ongoing.

EMLM Mayor, Madoda Papiyana, commended the businessman for his vigilance and emphasized the municipality’s zero-tolerance stance on infrastructure theft.

“We are pleased that the police were alerted promptly and that an arrest was made. We are in a daily battle against the theft and vandalism of our electricity infrastructure, and our pursuit does not stop with the individuals caught on the ground,” Papiyana said.

The mayor wants to target the masterminds, buyers, sellers, and entire syndicate profiting from this destruction, stating there’s a thriving black market dismantling government-built infrastructure.

“That entire chain must be broken. Every time a cable is stolen or a transformer vandalised, our residents suffer through power interruptions. This is not just theft, it’s an attack on our community, and we will not tolerate it,” he concluded.

A case has been with the suspect expected to appear in the Komani magistrate court by next week.

Residents can report suspicious activity to the Municipal Control Room on 045 839 2181

The post Suspect arrrested for cable theft in Queendustria appeared first on The Rep.
